q: Gottman's research shows enduring couples keep detailed love maps — a constantly updated knowledge of each other's inner world.
who: Ask one curious question about each other's day
why: You can never finish learning a person. Their inner world changes every day. One curious question keeps the map current.

q: Partners who keep learning about each other resist the 'I already know you' trap.
who: Note something new in each other's inner world
why: The 'I already know you' trap kills long relationships quietly. Curiosity keeps love from going stale. Keep asking as if you just met.

q: Gottman found masters turn toward bids for attention about 86% of the time, versus 33% for couples who later separated.
who: Respond to small bids instead of half-ignoring
why: Love lives in tiny moments — a sigh, a 'look at this'. Masters turn toward them; others turn away. Notice the bid, and answer it.

q: The first three minutes of a disagreement strongly predict how it ends.
why: How a conversation starts is usually how it ends. A soft opening invites a soft reply. Begin gently, even when upset.

q: Repair attempts are the single biggest difference between stable and unstable couples.
why: Stable couples aren't the ones who never fight. They're the ones who reach out mid-fight. A joke, a touch, a 'let's start over' saves the day.

q: Closing on common ground prevents the unresolved residue that quietly accumulates into resentment over months.
who: End hard talks with one point of agreement
why: Never end a hard talk with nothing agreed. Even one small point of agreement clears the residue. Resentment grows in what's left unresolved.

q: Reflecting proves you actually received your partner — the core of perceived responsiveness.
who: Reflect back what your partner said before replying
why: Repeat back what you heard before you answer. Being understood matters more than being agreed with. It proves the message truly arrived.

q: A small concrete action outperforms reassurance because it shows you were listening and you followed through.
who: Do one small thing your partner mentioned needing
why: Reassurance is words; action is proof. One small deed shows you were really listening. Do the thing, don't just promise comfort.

q: Undivided attention is the cheapest and rarest gift you can give in a distracted age.
who: Put the phone down when your partner is talking
why: Full attention is rare in a distracted age. Even two minutes of it says: you matter most. Put the phone down and look up.

q: Small kept promises build a felt sense of safety far more than occasional grand gestures.
who: Do what you said you'd do
why: Trust isn't built in speeches. It's built in small promises kept, again and again. Grand gestures can't replace daily reliability.

q: A partner who can be reached when needed becomes a secure base.
why: Answer when it counts. A partner who can be reached becomes a safe harbour. Availability is love made practical.

q: Quick repair teaches the relationship a vital lesson: bad moments are survivable.
who: Name and fix any small rupture same-day
why: Fix the small scratch today so it never becomes a scar. Quick repair teaches you both that bad moments pass. The relationship learns it is safe.

q: Rituals create reliable points of contact that survive busy weeks.
why: One tiny ritual holds a couple through busy weeks. The morning coffee, the goodnight text. Reliability is romance in disguise.

q: Couples with a shared sense of direction report more meaning and more resilience under stress.
why: Talk about where the two of you are going. A shared direction carries you through stress. Meaning is made together, not found alone.

q: Studies link natural 'we'-talk to higher satisfaction and better conflict recovery.
why: Say 'we' more than 'you and I'. Language quietly shapes how a couple thinks. Teams recover from conflict faster than opponents.

q: The Arons' self-expansion research shows novel shared activity reignites the excitement that routine quietly erodes.
why: Try one new thing together. Novelty re-creates the excitement of the early days. The brain can't quite tell adventure from falling in love.

q: Bringing new ideas into the relationship keeps each of you interesting to the other — a steady defence against the slide into boredom.
why: Share what's growing in you. Staying interesting is a gift to the relationship. Two evolving people never run out of things to discover.

q: Deliberate novelty resists hedonic adaptation — the slow numbing to good things we already have, including a good partner.
why: We slowly go numb to good things we already have. Breaking routine on purpose resets the senses. Surprise is maintenance, not luxury.

q: Specific gratitude — naming the actual act — makes a partner feel truly seen, and predicts higher commitment far more than a vague 'thanks'.
why: Thank them for the specific thing, by name. Vague gratitude bounces off; precise gratitude lands. Being seen in detail is being loved in detail.

q: Naming effort that usually goes unseen directly counters the drift toward taking each other for granted — a top quiet killer of relationships.
why: Point out the effort that usually goes unseen. Feeling invisible is how love quietly erodes. Naming the work reverses the drift.

q: Expressed admiration builds fondness — the buffer Gottman found protects couples through their hardest seasons.
who: Tell your partner one thing you admire
why: Say what you admire out loud. Spoken fondness becomes armour for hard seasons. Silent admiration protects no one.

q: Stress spillover is well documented: tension carried in from outside gets discharged onto the nearest person.
why: Tension from outside gets discharged on the nearest person. Leave the day at the door. Your partner is your refuge, not your outlet.

q: Buffering couple time from outside pressure preserves the relationship's repair capacity — the reserve you draw on when things get hard.
why: Guard a pocket of time that belongs only to you two. Outside pressure takes everything it is allowed to. Protected time keeps repair possible.

q: Sharing a hard day builds intimacy; making a partner absorb it erodes it.
who: Don't dump work stress on each other
why: Share the hard day; don't hand it over to carry. Telling builds intimacy, dumping erodes it. Let them stand beside you, not beneath the load.

q: A hand on the shoulder is a small daily biological deposit of safety.
why: A hand on the shoulder in passing says 'I'm here'. Touch makes small daily deposits of safety. The body keeps score of tenderness too.

q: Sincere compliments sustain positive sentiment — the lens through which neutral or clumsy acts later get read kindly instead of harshly.
why: Sincere compliments build kind eyes. Through them, clumsy moments get read charitably. Positive sentiment is the lens everything passes through.

q: A roughly 20-second hug or 6-second kiss crosses the threshold where bonding chemistry actually engages.
why: Hold the hug a little longer. Around twenty seconds, bonding chemistry actually kicks in. The body needs time to believe it's safe.

q: People who see relationships as cultivated persist through rough patches; 'soulmate' believers quit sooner when reality gets difficult.
who: Treat friction as buildable, not fatal
why: Love isn't found, it's grown. Growers persist through the rough patches. Soulmate-believers quit when the magic fades — gardeners replant.

q: Relationships are maintained by accumulated small choices, not sustained by feeling alone.
why: Feelings rise and fall on their own schedule. Choices are the part you control. One small deliberate act a day outweighs waiting to feel like it.

q: A charitable read of ambiguous behaviour breaks the negative-interpretation spiral in which everything a partner does gets read as a slight.
why: When in doubt, assume they meant well. It's usually true — and it breaks the negative spiral. Suspicion, once habitual, finds evidence everywhere.

q: No coldness, no silence as punishment. We ask directly and kindly.
why: Silence used as a weapon wounds deeper than words. Ask directly, kindly, even mid-disagreement. Warmth is not a reward — it's the rule.

q: No unresolved hurt goes to sleep with us.
why: Never let a hurt spend the night unspoken. It grows in the dark. Morning always finds it bigger.

q: Small and early, spoken or written. No suffering alone overnight.
why: Say it while it's still small. Spoken or written, but never carried alone overnight. Waiting turns a pebble into a stone.

q: Fully accepted, history included. It never enters a disagreement.
why: What's been accepted stays accepted. Old chapters never enter new disagreements. The past is closed — it has earned its peace.

q: Neither pressure nor retreat.
why: Move at the speed of the slower heart. No pushing forward, no pulling away. Together is the only pace.

q: Phones away, fully present.
why: One evening a week where nothing else exists. Phones away, fully present. Attention, undivided, is the whole point.

q: Unhurried, private, listening to each other's bodies.
why: Intimacy grows from safety, never from hurry. Listen to each other's bodies. Privacy and patience do the rest.

q: We show up loudly for good news, not just faithfully for bad.
why: Show up loudly for good news, not just faithfully for bad. Celebration is a form of loyalty. How you receive joy predicts more than how you handle sorrow.

q: Gratitude unspoken doesn't count.
why: Gratitude unspoken doesn't count. Felt thanks helps only the one who feels it. Say it, or it never happened.

q: Review, adjust, keep building.
why: Every few months, look at the relationship itself. Tend the garden, not just the flowers. What gets reviewed gets to keep growing.

q: Kate Muir's How to Have a Magnificent Midlife Crisis, distilled. Eighteen chapters with questions to read together.
why: The middle years taken as invitation, not crisis. Eighteen chapters, distilled to what's usable. A map for the second half.

q: The science of desire that lasts — Perel, Nagoski, Aron, Mark & Lasslo. Seven practices to keep the flame alive.
why: Desire that lasts is a practice, not an accident. Perel, Nagoski, Aron — the science agrees. Seven practices keep the spark deliberate.

q: An interactive dice-and-card game for two. Six tiers, 216 unique card-experiences. For one evening with no rush.
why: Play is how grown-ups stay young together. 216 card-experiences, one unrushed evening. Roll the dice and see who you become.
